title: Power BI - Metrics, Goals, And Scorecards
applies_to: Power BI scorecards and KPI tracking
Business objective tracking in Power BI service

confirmed_facts.fact: The Power BI learning path includes a goals and metrics scenario focused on scorecards, connected goals or metrics, subgoals, check-ins, status rules, permissions, subscriptions, and Power Automate automation.
Power BI scorecards and manual metrics let users track key business objectives in a single pane and can be shared with others in the Power BI service.
Authoring and sharing scorecards in standard workspaces requires a Power BI Pro license plus Admin, Member, or Contributor role in a workspace and Build permission for a semantic model.
Power BI automatically saves the scorecard and an associated semantic model that houses the metrics data in the selected workspace.
Metrics can be entered manually or connected to existing report visuals, and scorecards support submetrics or subgoals and configurable tracking cycles.

implementation_notes: Use scorecards when the user wants KPI tracking and status management, not just analytic report pages.
Treat metrics and scorecards as service-managed artifacts with their own sharing and permission model, separate from normal report authoring.
If the goal is operational follow-up, consider the Power Automate branch from the learning path after the scorecard design is stable.
caveats: Scorecards are not a substitute for detailed report analysis; they are a management layer on top of data and progress tracking.
Workspace role and semantic-model Build permission are easy to overlook in scorecard implementations.
Terminology varies across Microsoft Learn pages between metrics and goals, so implementations should normalize on the terminology used in the current tenant UI.
